hook: You already know how to graph a line and recognize its slope and y-intercept — that's a powerful start. But what happens when you're handed two points, a slope and one point, or a real-world data table instead of a neat equation? This unit gives you the tools to build the equation of any line from almost any information you're given.
concept: Every topic in this unit is about translating information — two points, a slope, a data trend, or a number pattern — into the equation of a line. The unifying idea is that a linear relationship can be expressed in multiple equivalent forms, and choosing the right form makes problem-solving faster and cleaner.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to write the equation of a line in the form $y = mx + b$ directly from a slope and y-intercept or from a graph.
Master writing a line's equation as $y - y_1 = m(x - x_1)$ when you're given a slope and any single point — no y-intercept required.
Apply your skills to rewrite linear equations in the form $Ax + By = C$, recognizing when this form is the most useful way to present a solution.
You will learn to use the relationship between slopes — equal slopes for parallel lines and negative reciprocal slopes for perpendicular lines — to write equations of related lines.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Scatter Plots and Lines of Best Fit
topic_roadmap.description: Apply linear equation writing to real-world data by drawing and interpreting a line of best fit that models the trend in a scatter plot.
You will learn to recognize arithmetic sequences as linear functions and write their explicit rules by connecting the common difference to slope and the first term to the y-intercept.
